# Abstract

Aircraft cabin and signaling lighting systems must function across a wide range of supply voltages and increasingly require modularity, accurate dimming, and little wiring. This thesis proposes and validates the digital subsystem of a daisy-chainable LED driver IC concept intended for aircraft lighting segments supplied from 18–36 V. In order to maintain human-perceived simultaneity, the baseline device aims for at least four independent channels (RGBW), 16-bit dimming precision, low-side PWM, and per-channel adjustable LED current. It also makes sure that even the 25th segment in a chain receives and implements new settings within 25 ms. The internal requirements and implementation report and AES's concept wishlist serve as the basis for these system goals and constraints. The latter also outlines a UART-based segment-to-segment protocol (9N1 at 1 MBd) that uses start- and end-of-frame markers in which each segment uses its own bytes and forwards the remaining ones downstream.

The primary contribution of this thesis is the design and FPGA-based verification of the Digital Control & Forwarding Subsystem which includes the receiver/transmitter, register architecture, framing and parsing logic, 16-bit PWM generation, and deterministic forwarding. Simulink is used for early architectural exploration, latency modeling, PWM analysis, and scalability verification before hardware synthesis. .The implementation is prototyped on the Cologne Chip GateMate FPGA  utilising an open toolchain such as Yosys and nextpnr for GateMate FPGA validation. Additionally, University Chat AI is employed to explore multiple large language models (LLMs) as a productivity aid for RTL development, verification, and documentation; all AI-assisted outputs are validated through simulation and on-hardware testing. The result is an ASIC-ready digital architecture with quantified timing, scalability, and functional correctness aligned to the stated requirements.

## Keywords

LED driver IC, aircraft lighting, daisy chain, UART framing, 16-bit PWM, low-side switching, GateMate FPGA, Yosys, nextpnr, openFPGALoader, open-source EDA, RTL design.
